claims: Go defines happens-before via synchronization: goroutine creation, channel send/receive, mutex lock/unlock ordering, sync.Once, and atomics.
Data-race-free Go programs execute as if sequentially consistent (DRF-SC).

Go constrains compilers explicitly: no introducing new writes, no single read observing multiple values, no single write writing multiple values, no moving accesses out of possibly non-terminating loops, no moving accesses before calls unless proven side-effect/synchronization free.
Racy programs may observe non-sequentially-consistent behavior; idioms like double-checked locking and busy-wait flags are invalid without synchronization.
caveats: Go bounds racy-program behavior more than C++ (implementation may also report races and halt); constraints are on implementations, not a claim that racy code is meaningful.

claims: Rust pragmatically inherits the C++ atomic memory model with SeqCst, Acquire/Release, and Relaxed orderings.
Compilers may radically reorder or eliminate ordinary data accesses based on single-threaded analysis, so unsynchronized data accesses cannot build correct synchronization.
Hardware ordering strength differs (x86 strong, ARM weak); atomic orderings control which compiler/hardware reorderings are forbidden.
caveats: Nomicon notes the model is complex with known flaws; SeqCst is often stronger than needed and can cost fences.

claims: Send means safe to transfer to another thread; Sync means safe to share (&T is Send).
Send/Sync are unsafe auto traits: automatically derived from components, with raw pointers, UnsafeCell, and Rc as key exceptions.
Send/Sync plus the borrow checker prevent data races in safe code; incorrect manual implementations are undefined behavior.
caveats: They do not prevent deadlocks or logic errors; manual unsafe impls shift proof burden to the programmer.

claims: LLVM defines ordering levels NotAtomic, Unordered, Monotonic, Acquire, Release, AcquireRelease, SequentiallyConsistent with distinct optimizer freedoms.
A racing non-atomic load returns undef rather than full UB at the IR level; speculative loads are allowed.
Optimizers may not insert stores to possibly-shared variables along paths where they would not otherwise occur.
Atomic operations forbid splitting/narrowing/rematerialization transformations; specific passes (DSE, LICM, CSE) retain limited legal transforms around weaker orderings.
caveats: IR-level semantics differ from source-language semantics; per-pass behavior varies and must be checked individually.

claims: Modern languages converge on DRF-SC: data-race-free programs behave sequentially consistently.
C/C++/Rust/Swift treat races as undefined behavior; Java/JavaScript bound racy outcomes for security reasons.
Compilers may reorder ordinary accesses within single-threaded semantics but cannot introduce new racy reads/writes; atomics/volatile restrict register caching.
Out-of-thin-air values remain a formally unresolved problem in all these models.
caveats: Survey/essay by Russ Cox: authoritative synthesis but secondary source; language versions evolve.

claims: Java compilers/JITs/processors may reorder within as-if-serial single-thread semantics, but cannot move code before an acquire or after a release.
Volatile write/read have monitor release/acquire memory semantics and cannot reorder with each other or with surrounding accesses (post-JSR-133).
Final fields provide initialization safety without synchronization if the object reference does not escape construction.
Values must not appear out of thin air; empty synchronized blocks on unshared monitors may be removed.
caveats: FAQ (2004) is explanatory, not the JLS text itself; details refined in later JLS versions.
